topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery opening was a suffocating affair, a parade of self-congratulatory smiles and vapid chatter. I stood near my latest canvas, a swirling depiction of urban decay rendered in stark, unforgiving lines. Then I saw it. On the wrist of the woman across the room, stark black ink coiled around her forearm, mimicking the very texture of my latest piece. A slow, cold knot tightened in my chest. I forced a polite nod toward her, my gaze deliberately skimming over the intricate design, cataloging its amateurish execution. It felt like a deliberate, clumsy appropriation, an insult thinly veiled as homage. I retreated to the relative quiet of the corner, the air suddenly tasting metallic.

[story 2]

"Remarkable technique," a voice purred from the crowd. I barely registered the compliment. My eyes snagged on the subject of the praise—a portrait I had just finished, a study in muted chiaroscuro. Etched onto the nape of the subject's neck, almost hidden by the curve of her hair, was a miniature, almost perfect rendering of my composition. A sharp, unpleasant heat rose in my neck. I straightened my spine, my jaw clenching so tightly my molars ached. I didn't look at the person who had done it; instead, I focused on the impeccable symmetry of my own brushstrokes, a silent, furious rebuttal simmering just beneath my skin.

[story 3]

I found the evidence tucked beneath a loose floorboard in the studio—a small, faded sketch, undeniably mine, but overlaid with a precise, almost mocking tattoo design. My breath hitched, a shallow, sharp intake of air. The feeling that washed over me was heavy, like wet concrete settling on my shoulders. I picked up the paper, my fingers trembling slightly, not from excitement, but from a profound sense of being undermined. I crumpled the paper slowly, deliberately, the sound a small, brittle snap in the silence of the workspace. I needed distance. I needed to scrub the intrusion from my immediate vicinity, to erase the unwelcome familiarity.
